name.text: Week 13 – Q1: Lumped vs Distributed Model Identification

questiontext.text:
Lumped vs Distributed Model Identification

Course: Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

This is a training exercise — you may use books, tutors, and AI. The question requires multi-step reasoning.

Total: 9 points (9 parts, 1 pt each) | Penalty per wrong attempt: 0%

A transmission line of length \(\ell = {@l_cm@}\;\text{cm}\) carries a signal at frequency \(f = {@f_disp@}\). The phase velocity on the line is \(v_p = {@vp_disp@} \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}\).

To decide whether a lumped-element or distributed (transmission-line) model is appropriate, we compare the physical length of the line to the signal wavelength. The rule of thumb is:

If \(\displaystyle \frac{\ell}{\lambda} \ge 0.01\), use the distributed model; otherwise the lumped model suffices.

Part (a) — Wavelength (1 pt)

Compute the wavelength \(\lambda = v_p / f\). Give your answer in metres.

\(\lambda = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]] m

Hint: \(\lambda = v_p / f\). Enter a numerical value (scientific notation accepted, e.g. 2e5).

Part (b) — Electrical length ratio (1 pt)

Compute the ratio \(\ell / \lambda\).

\(\ell / \lambda = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]]

Hint: Dimensionless ratio. Scientific notation accepted.

Part (c) — Threshold check (1 pt)

Is \(\ell / \lambda \ge 0.01\)?

[[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]]

Select Yes or No from the dropdown.

Part (d) — Model selection (1 pt)

Based on your answer above, which model is appropriate at frequency \(f = {@f_disp@}\)?

[[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]]

Select Lumped or Distributed from the dropdown.

Part (e) — Higher frequency (1 pt)

Now suppose the frequency is increased by a factor of 100 (i.e. \(f_{\text{new}} = 100\,f\)), while the line length and phase velocity remain the same. Compute the new ratio \(\ell / \lambda_{\text{new}}\).

\(\ell / \lambda_{\text{new}} = \) [[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]]

Hint: How does wavelength change when frequency increases? Scientific notation accepted.

Part (f) — Model at higher frequency (1 pt)

At the higher frequency, which model is appropriate?

[[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

Select Lumped or Distributed from the dropdown.

Part (g) — Physical insight (1 pt)

Which statement best explains why increasing the frequency can change the modelling paradigm from lumped to distributed?

[[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

Select the most physically accurate explanation.

Part (h) — Design: minimum frequency for distributed model (1 pt)

Suppose you are designing a system using this same interconnect (length \(\ell = {@l_cm@}\;\text{cm}\), phase velocity \(v_p = {@vp_disp@} \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}\)). What is the minimum signal frequency at which you must use the distributed model?

Use the same threshold: \(\ell/\lambda \ge 0.01\). Give your answer in Hz.

\(f_{\min} = \) [[input:ans8]] [[validation:ans8]] Hz

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 2e7. Scientific notation accepted.

Part (i) — Sensitivity: what if the phase velocity is lower? (1 pt)

Your calculated \(f_{\min} = {@ta8_disp@}\) assumes a phase velocity of \(v_p = {@vp_disp@} \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}\).

In practice, the actual phase velocity depends on the dielectric fill, manufacturing tolerances, and temperature. If the true phase velocity turns out to be 20% lower than assumed, how does this affect your design decision at the original frequency \(f = {@f_disp@}\)?

[[input:ans9]] [[validation:ans9]]

Select the most accurate statement about the engineering implication.

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Part (a) — Wavelength

The wavelength is:

\[\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{{@vp@}}{{@f_Hz@}} = {@ta1@}\;\text{m}\]

Part (b) — Electrical length ratio

The ratio of line length to wavelength:

\[\frac{\ell}{\lambda} = \frac{{@l_m@}}{{@ta1@}} = {@ta2@}\]

Part (c) — Threshold check

We compare \(\ell/\lambda = {@ta2@}\) to the threshold \(0.01\).

Since \(\ell/\lambda {@if is_distributed then "\\ge" else "<"@} 0.01\), the answer is {@if is_distributed then "Yes" else "No"@}.

Part (d) — Model selection

Since \(\ell/\lambda {@if is_distributed then "\\ge" else "<"@} 0.01\), we use the {@if is_distributed then "Distributed" else "Lumped"@} model.

Part (e) — Higher frequency

When frequency increases by a factor of 100, the wavelength shrinks by 100, so \(\ell/\lambda\) increases by 100:

\[\frac{\ell}{\lambda_{\text{new}}} = 100 \times {@ta2@} = {@ta5@}\]

Part (f) — Model at higher frequency

Now \(\ell/\lambda_{\text{new}} = {@ta5@}\), which is {@if is_distributed_high then "\\ge" else "<"@} \(0.01\), so the {@if is_distributed_high then "Distributed" else "Lumped"@} model applies.

Part (g) — Physical insight

The correct explanation is: At higher frequencies, the wavelength shrinks and becomes comparable to the line length, so wave propagation effects matter.

The key idea is that when the physical length of the line is a significant fraction of the wavelength, voltages and currents vary along the line and the lumped approximation (uniform V and I) breaks down.

Part (h) — Minimum frequency for distributed model

We need \(\ell/\lambda \ge 0.01\). Since \(\lambda = v_p/f\), this gives:

\[\frac{\ell}{v_p/f} \ge 0.01 \implies f \ge \frac{0.01 \cdot v_p}{\ell} = \frac{0.01 \times {@vp@}}{{@l_m@}} = {@ta8@}\;\text{Hz}\]

Part (i) — Sensitivity to phase velocity

If the true phase velocity is 20% lower, then:

\[f_{\min,\text{new}} = \frac{0.01 \times (0.8 \, v_p)}{\ell} = 0.8 \times f_{\min} = 0.8 \times {@ta8@} = {@ta8_low@}\;\text{Hz}\]

A lower \(v_p\) gives a lower \(f_{\min}\) — the distributed model threshold moves down in frequency. This means our original calculation was conservative (safe): the actual transition to the distributed regime happens at a lower frequency than we assumed, so any system designed using the original \(f_{\min}\) has an extra safety margin.

Answer Summary

PartAnswer
(a) λ{@ta1@} m
(b) l/λ{@ta2@}
(c) ≥ 0.01?{@if is_distributed then "Yes" else "No"@}
(d) Model{@if is_distributed then "Distributed" else "Lumped"@}
(e) l/λ at 100f{@ta5@}
(f) Model at 100f{@if is_distributed_high then "Distributed" else "Lumped"@}
(g) InsightWavelength shrinks, becomes comparable to line length
(h) fmin{@ta8@} Hz
(i) SensitivityConservative (safe) — f_min decreases

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — Wavelength

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b) — Electrical length ratio

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (c) — Threshold check

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (d) — Model selection

[[feedback:prt4]]

Part (e) — Higher frequency ratio

[[feedback:prt5]]

Part (f) — Model at higher frequency

[[feedback:prt6]]

Part (g) — Physical insight

[[feedback:prt7]]

Part (h) — Minimum frequency

[[feedback:prt8]]

Part (i) — Sensitivity

[[feedback:prt9]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: f={@f_disp@}, l={@l_cm@} cm, vp={@vp_disp@}×10⁸ m/s | λ={@ta1@} m, l/λ={@ta2@} | f_min={@ta8@} Hz | ta8_low={@ta8_low@}

questiondescription.text: Lumped vs distributed model identification — Week 13 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: Correct! \(\ell/\lambda = {@ta2@}\) is {@if is_distributed then "greater than or equal to" else "less than"@} 0.01.
Correct! At this frequency, the {@if is_distributed then "distributed" else "lumped"@} model is appropriate.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Since \(\ell/\lambda = {@ta2@}\) is {@if is_distributed then "≥" else "<"@} 0.01, the {@if is_distributed then "distributed" else "lumped"@} model applies.
Incorrect. When frequency increases by 100, wavelength decreases by 100, so \(\ell/\lambda\) increases by 100: \({@ta2@} \times 100 = {@ta5@}\).

node.description: Check part (f): model at higher frequency

truefeedback.text: Correct! At the higher frequency, the {@if is_distributed_high then "distributed" else "lumped"@} model is appropriate.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. At the higher frequency, \(\ell/\lambda_{\text{new}} = {@ta5@}\), which is {@if is_distributed_high then "≥" else "<"@} 0.01, so the {@if is_distributed_high then "distributed" else "lumped"@} model applies.

truefeedback.text: Correct! When frequency increases, the wavelength decreases. Once the line length becomes a significant fraction of the wavelength, voltage and current vary along the line and wave propagation effects (reflections, standing waves) become important.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The correct reasoning is: at higher frequencies the wavelength shrinks and becomes comparable to the line length, so wave propagation effects (reflections, standing waves, spatial variation of V and I) become significant, requiring the distributed model.

truefeedback.text: Correct! The minimum frequency for the distributed model is \(f_{\min} = {@ta8@}\;\text{Hz}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. From the threshold \(\ell/\lambda \ge 0.01\) and \(\lambda = v_p/f\), solve for \(f\): \(f_{\min} = 0.01 \cdot v_p / \ell = {@ta8@}\;\text{Hz}\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! A lower phase velocity gives a lower \(f_{\min}\), making the original design conservative. The safety margin increases because the distributed regime starts at an even lower frequency than you calculated.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Since \(f_{\min} = 0.01 \cdot v_p / \ell\), a lower \(v_p\) gives a lower \(f_{\min}\). This means the distributed model becomes necessary at a lower frequency — the original calculation overestimates the threshold, making the design conservative (safe).

hint.text: Hint 1: The wavelength of a signal on a transmission line is \(\lambda = v_p / f\), where \(v_p\) is the phase velocity and \(f\) is the frequency. Start by computing this value.

Hint 2: The rule of thumb for choosing between lumped and distributed models is based on the ratio \(\ell/\lambda\). If this ratio is at least 0.01 (i.e. the line is at least 1% of a wavelength long), the distributed model is needed because voltage and current vary appreciably along the line.

Hint 3: When the frequency increases by a factor of 100, the wavelength decreases by the same factor (since \(\lambda = v_p/f\) and \(v_p\) is unchanged). Therefore \(\ell/\lambda\) increases by 100. This can push a line that was safely "lumped" into the distributed regime — the paradigm shift occurs because the physical length has not changed, but the electrical length (measured in wavelengths) has grown.

For part (h), rearrange the threshold condition \(\ell/\lambda \ge 0.01\) with \(\lambda = v_p/f\) to solve for the minimum frequency \(f\).

For part (i), recall that \(f_{\min} = 0.01 \cdot v_p / \ell\). If \(v_p\) decreases, what happens to \(f_{\min}\)? Think about whether this makes your original design decision more or less safe.
